coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots electrochemically prepared from dopamine and epigallocatechin gallate for hypochlorite detection with high selectivity via a dynamic quenching mechanism
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Long, Tiantian
Hu, Zhongyang
Gao, Ziyun
Luo, Hongmei
Li, Hongchen
Chen, Yi
Liu, Lei
Xu, Dong
coredata.dc:description: Monitoring hypochlorite levels in water is of great importance because of its high toxicity and wide applications as water disinfectants. In this manuscript, carbon dot (CD) was electrochemically prepared by using dopamine and epigallocatechin gallate (molar ratio 1:1) as the carbon source for efficient hypochlorite determination. By electrolyzing the solution at 10 V for 12 min with PBS as an electrolyte, dopamine would react with epigallocatechin at the anode, and through polymerization, dehydration, and carbonization, strong blue-fluorescent CDs were obtained. CDs were characterized by UV–Vis spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy, FT-IR, etc. These CDs have an excitation wavelength at 372 nm and an emission wavelength at 462 nm, owing an average particle size of 5.5 nm. The presence of hypochlorites can quench the fluorescence of CDs, and its reduction in intensity is linear with hypochlorite concentration over the range of 0.5–50 μM, ΔF/F0 = 0.0056 + 0.0194CClO −, R2=0.997. The detection limit achieved 0.23 μM (S/N = 3). The mechanism for fluorescence quenching is via a dynamic process. Different from many other fluorescence methods based on the strong oxidizing ability of hypochlorites, our method shows strong selectivity toward hypochlorites over other oxidizing agents such as H2O2. The assay was validated by the detection of hypochlorites in water samples, with recoveries between 98.2% and 104.3%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Hypochlorite
Fluorescence
Electrochemical method
Dynamic quenching

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Feasibility of sensing ClO− by using B-CDs and the mechanism
. The validation of our assay in water sample detection

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by funding from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21505162, 31571874), Hunan Provincial Key Laboratory of Food Safety Monitoring, Early Warning (2020KFJJ02), Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province, China (2020RC3042, 2022SK2102), the Grain-Oil Process and Quality Control 2011 Collaborative and Innovative Grant from Hunan province, and Training Program for Excellent Young Innovators of Changsha (kq2009060).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–Vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectrum of B-CDs. The inset is images of the DP+EGCG mixed solution under natural light (Up) and 365 nm UV lamp (bottom) before (left) and after (right) electrolysis. (b) HR-TEM image and particle size distribution statistics. (c) Raman spectrum. (d) FT-IR spectra.
Total XPS spectrum (a) and high-resolution XPS spectra of (b) C1s, (c) N1s, (d) O1s of B-CDs.
Fluorescence spectra of B-CDs without and with 50 µM ClO− (λex = 372 nm; λem = 462 nm; Slit = 5.0 nm). The inset is the corresponding images under UV light.
(a) FT-IR spectra of B-CDs without and with the presence of ClO−. Total XPS spectrum (b) and high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s (c), N1s (d), and O1s (e) of B-CDs before and after reaction with ClO−.
The impact of solution pH (a), temperature (b), and incubation time (c) on the detection of ClO−.
(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of B-CDs with increasing ClO− concentration over 0–300 μM, (b) ΔF/F0 is linear with ClO− concentration over the range of 0.5–50 μM.
Selectivity of B-CDs for ClO− sensing over other 2-fold anions and cations including Cr2O7 2−, IO3 −, SO4 2−, C7H6O2 −, CH3COO–, Br−, H2O2, NO3 –, CO3 2–, K+, NH4 +, Zn2+, and Pb2+.
Schematic illustration of B-CDs electrochemically prepared from DP and EGCG for ClO− detection.
Comparison of CD-based methods for hypochlorite determination.
Application of B-CDs for hypochlorite detection in water samples.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots electrochemically prepared from dopamine and epigallocatechin gallate for hypochlorite detection with high selectivity via a dynamic quenching mechanism

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The first three authors contributed equally.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Monitoring hypochlorite levels in water is of great importance because of its high toxicity and wide applications as water disinfectants. In this manuscript, carbon dot (CD) was electrochemically prepared by using dopamine and epigallocatechin gallate (molar ratio 1:1) as the carbon source for efficient hypochlorite determination. By electrolyzing the solution at 10 V for 12 min with PBS as an electrolyte, dopamine would react with epigallocatechin at the anode, and through polymerization, dehydration, and carbonization, strong blue-fluorescent CDs were obtained. CDs were characterized by UV–Vis spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy, FT-IR, etc. These CDs have an excitation wavelength at 372 nm and an emission wavelength at 462 nm, owing an average particle size of 5.5 nm. The presence of hypochlorites can quench the fluorescence of CDs, and its reduction in intensity is linear with hypochlorite concentration over the range of 0.5–50 μM, ΔF/F0 = 0.0056 + 0.0194CClO −, R2=0.997. The detection limit achieved 0.23 μM (S/N = 3). The mechanism for fluorescence quenching is via a dynamic process. Different from many other fluorescence methods based on the strong oxidizing ability of hypochlorites, our method shows strong selectivity toward hypochlorites over other oxidizing agents such as H2O2. The assay was validated by the detection of hypochlorites in water samples, with recoveries between 98.2% and 104.3%.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Hypochlorite (ClO−) is an inorganic chlorinated chemical, being widely used in water purification [1,2], agricultural product storage and preservation [3,4], pharmaceutical industry [5–7], and environmental disinfection [8]. Especially, hypochlorites are the actual effective ingredients to eliminate contamination from bacteria and other microorganisms in tap water [9]. Their content can be an index for water disinfection and recontamination [10]. Too low concentration may lead the water pollution again, while too high contents would cause the smell of chlorine and health problems to human body. Hypochlorite accumulation in the body will inhibit the uptake of iodine by sodium and iodine co-transporters, thereby damaging thyroid function [11], and may also damage the immune system, leading to a variety of diseases, such as liver ischemia-reperfusion injury, atherosclerosis, lung injury, rheumatoid disease, cardiovascular disease, neuronal degeneration, and arthritis [12]. It was also proposed that hypochlorites can cause carcinogenic, teratogenic, and mutagenic risks [13]. At the same time, a high concentration of hypochlorites in drinking water could be converted into non-degradable trihalomethanes as by-products, which further threaten human health to a large extent [14,15]. Therefore, sensitive and selective detection of hypochlorites in water and biological samples is quite requirable.

At present, the detection techniques for hypochlorites include titration, gas chromatography-flame ionization detection, electrochemical method, and fluorescence technique [11,16,17]. Among these techniques, fluorescence-based detection methods have attracted wider interest because of their high sensitivity, low background, and strong anti-interference properties. In the past, hypochlorite detection typically relied on the design of organic probes. For example, Yang [18] developed a new fluorescent probe for the “turn-on” sensing of ClO−, resulting in the fluorescence turning from dark to bright cyan, with a linear detection range of 1–180 μM. However, these organic molecules suffer from photobleaching, low stability, and high cost. Carbon dot (CD) with superior properties such as strong fluorescence, tunable emission wavelength, high stability, easy fabrication, and low toxicity emerged as an innovative optical probe in analytical fields [19–22]. A few types of CDs have been developed for hypochlorite sensing. For instance, Qi et al introduced nitrogen-doped CDs with strong blue fluorescence showing sensitive and selective detection of hypochlorites with a detection limit of 0.43 μM [23]. Chen et al. introduced CDs co-doped with sulfur and nitrogen for high sensing of ClO− in solutions and MCF-7 cells [22]. Wang et al [24] reported fluorometric and colorimetric dual-mode detection of hypochlorites using boron and nitrogen doped CDs. However, these methods rely on the robust oxidizing capacity of hypochlorites, which means less immunity to interference from other oxidizing agents such as H2O2 and Cr2O7 2 −.

In this manuscript, blue-fluorescent carbon dots (B-CDs) were electrochemically prepared by using dopamine (DP) and epigallocatechin gallate (EGCG) as carbon precursors for the quantification of ClO−. At the anode DP would react with EGCG and the product was further carbonized to CDs through polymerization, dehydration, and carbonization. Neither DP nor EGCG could be converted into fluorescent CDs. The B-CDs have a good response to ClO− and its presence would quench the fluorescence of B-CDs via a dynamic process. More importantly, our sensing is not based on the strong oxidizing ability of ClO−, thus demonstrating strong anti-interference from H2O2 and Cr2O7 2−. Finally, our assay was validated in water sample detection.

DP (99%), EGCG (98%), and sodium hypochlorite (NaClO) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Other substances, including sodium benzoate, ammonium sulfate, zinc acetate, potassium iodate, potassium dichromate, lead nitrate, sodium carbonate, hydrogen peroxide, and potassium bromide were bought from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). All mentioned reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification.

UV-2700 spectrometer (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan) was applied to capture the UV–Vis spectrum of solutions. Fluorescence emission and excitation spectra of B-CDs were recorded using a G9800A fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent, Santa Clara, CA, USA). The size and morphology were characterized by using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, Titan G2 60-300, FEI, USA). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was recorded by using an IR Tracer-100 spectrometer with KBr pellet from Shimadzu (Kyoto, Japan). The Zeta potential was measured on the Nano-zeta potential analyzer (Nano-ZS90, Malvern, UK). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was tested using ESCALAB Xi+ (Thermo Fischer, USA). The fluorescence lifetime and quantum yield of B-CDs were both performed by the FLS1000 from Edinburgh Instruments (Livingston, England, UK). Raman spectra were recorded on the Labram Aramis spectrometer with an excitation wavelength at 359 nm from HORIBA Scientific LabRAM HR Evolution (Palaiseau, France). B-CDs solution lyophilized at 48 °C and 150 mm Hg for 72 h was turned into powder (Kansas City, MO, USA).

0.2 mM of DP and 0.2 mM of EGCG were dissolved in 13 mL ultrapure water ultrasound. Then, 2 mL PBS buffer (0.2 mM, pH = 7) was added as an electrolyte. Two platinum cylindrical electrodes with a diameter of 0.1 cm and a length of 10 cm were immersed in the solution and 10 V voltage was applied for 12 min. Finally, pale yellow B-CD solution was obtained, which was dialyzed in ultrapure water for 12 h with a dialysis bag having a cut-off weight of 600 Da. The water was changed at least twice. The CDs were further characterized by UV–Vis spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, and HR-TEM.

In order to improve the sensitivity for hypochlorite detection, the effects of pH value (pH 2–13), temperature (10–50 °C), and incubation time were investigated. The purified B-CDs solution was diluted 5 times for hypochlorite detection. After mixing B-CDs with different concentrations of ClO− (0, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40, 45, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90, 100, 150, 200, 300 μM) in alkaline conditions (pH = 8) for 3 min. The fluorescence emission spectrum was scanned with an excitation wavelength at 372 nm (Slit:5.0 nm, voltage 550 V). To survey the selectivity, 60 μM of Cr2O7 2−, IO3 −, SO4 2−, C7H6O2 −, CH3COO–, Br−, H2O2, NO3 –, CO3 2–, K+, NH4 +, Zn2+, and Pb2+ were exploited instead for testing.

Water samples were collected from Changsha Houzishi Drinking Water Co., Ltd, Hunan, China, the student dormitory, and the ponds in our university. After filtering with a 0.2 μm membrane, the water samples were detected directly. 10, 30, and 50 μM spiked samples were made by extra adding hypochlorites. In brief, 2.5 mL B-CD solution was mixed with 0.5 mL PBS buffer (pH 8.0). Then, 0.5 mL of water sample was added, and after reaction for 3 min, fluorescence emission spectrum was recorded with the excitation wavelength at 372 nm. All the testing was repeated three times, and the recovery rate was calculated. The content of hypochlorites was also inspected by HED-AD01 portableresidualchlorinedetector (Shandong Holder Electronic Technology Co., Ltd).

We proposed a new type of B-CDs electrochemically prepared with DP and EGCG as carbon sources for sensitive detection of ClO−. At the anode, DP react with EGCG first to yield a product, which was subsequently carbonized to form fluorescent B-CDs (Scheme 1 ). The sole presence of DP or EGCG in solution did not yield strong fluorescent B-CDs, further confirming the reaction between DP and EGCG is indispensable for B-CD formation. It is reasonable that at the anode, water electrolysis would produce O2, which triggers the oxidization of EGCG, causing the subsequent reaction with DP. As reported, the dissolved O2 is indispensable for the reaction between DP and resorcinol to yield azamonardine [25] with strong fluorescence under pH 9–11. After polymerization, dehydration, and carbonization [19], strong fluorescent B-CDs were generated. The electrochemical method for CD fabrication is advantageous over the hydrothermal routines, without the need for high temperatures and long reaction time.

The optical properties of B-CDs were surveyed by using UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy (Fig. 1 a). The B-CDs aqueous solution has an apparent absorption peak located at 393 nm, which may correspond to the n-π* [26]. CDs possess a maximum excitation with wavelength at 372 nm and a superior emission at 462 nm (red line). The solution of DP and EGCG were mixed, being transparent and non-fluorescent. After electrolysis at 10 V, the color of the solution changes to bright yellow and emits strong blue fluorescence under a 365 nm UV lamp. The synthesis yield of the CDs is 18.32 ± 0.56% in mass, which is lower than the hydrothermal method [27], because not all the raw molecules participate in the reaction at the electrode. The absolute fluorescence quantum yield of B-CD solution is 0.53%. Fig. 1b shows that the B-CDs are nearly spherical and uniform. The sizes are distributed between 4 and 7 nm, with an average diameter of 5.5 nm. In the Raman spectra (Fig. 1c), two peaks at 1379 cm−1 and 1575 cm−1 are present, corresponding to D band and G band, respectively, which are associating with disordered or defective regions (D band) and sp2 carbon networks (G band) in the materials. The surface chemical groups were studied by FT-IR. Fig. 1d shows that the peak at 3420 cm−1 is O–H/N–H [28], which represents the hydrophilic functional group. The vibration of C–H [29] after electrolysis is located at 2850 cm−1. A new peak of 2470 cm−1 represents C-O [30], and the peak value of 1620 cm−1 is significantly higher than that before electrolysis, corresponding to CO vibration [31]. 1075 cm−1 and 1160 cm−1 correspond to the symmetric stretching vibration of -C-OH and –OH, indicating the presence of a large number of residual hydroxyl groups [32].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility of sensing ClO− by using B-CDs and the mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemically prepared B-CDs have a good response to ClO− after 5-fold dilution, as shown in Fig. 3 . The original B-CDs have a yellow colour and emit strong blue fluorescence with a distinct emission peak at 462 nm. After adding 50 µM of ClO− and incubation for 15 min, the fluorescence intensity decreased by 50%. Obviously, the fluorescence of B-CDs could be quenched by ClO− to a certain extent.

To further study the chemical structure of B-CDs with the presence of ClO−, FT-IR spectrum, and XPS spectra of B-CDs after an incubation with ClO− were obtained. Fig. 4 a shows that a broad peak 1610–1743 cm−1 after the reaction, which represents the absorption band generated by the interaction between B-CDs and ClO−. The XPS spectrum (Fig. 4b) shows that the signals at 200.4 eV, 284 eV, 400.1 eV, and 392.1 eV are Cl2p, C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. Compared with B-CDs, a new Cl2p emerged, which is due to the chemical reaction between B-CDs and ClO−. The contents of C1s and N1s increase, while that for O1s decreases significantly. Subsequently, the internal energy levels of C, N, and O elements were scanned in detail to determine chemical states (Fig. 4 c-e). Among them, 530.9 eV, 532 eV, and 535.5 belong to C-O, C-OH/C-O-C, and CO, respectively [32]. Further analysis of C1s shows that the values of 284, 285.5, and 288.4 eV are CC/CC, C-N/C-O, and CH, respectively [33], while the values of 398, 399.7, and 400.6 eV in N1s are corresponding to CN-C, pyrrolic-N, and C-N-C.

Fluorescence quenching process refers to the interaction between fluorescent dyes and quenchers , which is usually caused by dynamic or static quenching. Fluorescence lifetime is one of the effective means to judge the type of quenching. We, therefore, tested the fluorescence decay curves of B-CDs before and after the addition of ClO−. The calculated fluorescence lifetime of B-CDs before and after reaction with ClO− were 3.50 ns and 0.65 ns, respectively. The value changed significantly after incubation with ClO−. The response of B-CDs to ClO− was sensitive to temperature. When the temperature increased from 20 °C to 50 °C, the fluorescence intensity decreased by 56.7%, and increasing the temperature would raise the collision probability of particles in solution. Thus, a dynamic mechanism accounts for fluorescence quenching. The dynamic quenching process depends on the electron or energy transfer from B-CDs to ClO−. From the above test results, it can be inferred that the fluorescence sensing mechanism of B-CDs for ClO− is via dynamic quenching. The result seems contrary to that obtained from XPS and FT-IR, which support the direct reactions between CDs and ClO−. However, it is also reasonable that ClO− has strong oxidizing ability and negative charge, a small amount of which may react with the reducing chemical groups on the CD surface. Further research is needed to explain how they react.

For high performance of B-CDs to detect ClO−, different conditions i.e. solution pH, temperature, and incubation time were optimized (Fig. 5 ). It was found solution pH value was crucial for the detection (Fig. 5a). Under solution pH 2–13, the emission wavelength of B-CDs remained at 462 nm. At pH = 8, fluorescence quenching of B-CDs by 40 µM ClO− was the strongest. Especially, at low pH, excessive H+ could induce a formation of HClO, thus lowing ClO− ions concentration. In contrast, a higher pH value than 8, OH– would suppress the interaction between ClO− with B-CDs due to the charges. At different temperatures (10–50 °C), the fluorescence of B-CDs was recorded before and after the addition of ClO− (Fig. 5b). Temperature of 20 °C was better for sensing. The time-dependent fluorescence intensity at 462 nm was recorded immediately after hypochlorite addition (Fig. 5c). We found that B-CDs reacted with CIO− rapidly, and the fluorescence light intensity decreased by 70% within 30 s. Then, it slightly declined to 75% in 0.5–3 min, and finally remained stable after 3 min. Therefore, CIO− was quantitatively analyzed at 20 °C and pH 8 after incubation for 3 min.

Fig. 6 a shows the fluorescence emission spectra of B-CDs solutions with an excitation at 372 nm with the presence of various ClO− concentrations (0 to 300 µM) under the optimized conditions. The fluorescence intensity at the maximum emission at 462 nm was attained for hypochlorite determination. As the ClO− concentration increases, the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs decreases gradually. The variation of the fluorescence intensity at 462 nm depends on its concentrations linearly over the range of 0.5–50 µM. The linear regression for hypochlorite detection is ΔF/F0 = 0.0056 + 0.0194CClO − and the coefficient R2 = 0.997, where F0 is the intensity of original B-CDs at 462 nm, while ΔF is the intensity reduction. The limit of detection was calculated to be 0.23 µM (S/N = 3). In contrast to many CDs for hypochlorite determination, our method was based on dynamic quenching while others are based on the strongly oxidizing properties of ClO− [21,22,34]. It implies that the presence of other oxidizing agents such as H2O2 could lead to inaccurate detection. Moreover, our method offered a comparable sensitivity toward hypochlorite determination, which facilitates real sample detection (Table 1 ). Note that CDs used for hypochlorite detection are first prepared by a simple electrochemical method. Additionally, the extra addition of 0.01 M of NaBH4 solution could recover the fluorescence, again confirming the dynamic mechanism for quenching.

To evaluate the selectivity of B-CDs for ClO− detection over other substances, 2-fold of Cr2O7 2−, IO3 −, SO4 2−, C7H6O2 −, CH3COO–, Br−, H2O2, NO3 –, CO3 2–, K+, NH4 +, Zn2+, and Pb2+ were investigated instead of ClO− under the same conditions (Fig. 7 ). The anions with stronger oxidizing capacity and some cations were included. The results show these substances did not interfere with hypochlorite detection. Only CH3COO– and NO3 – showed a slight impact. We should emphasize that CrO7 2−, IO3 −, and H2O2 with strong oxidizing capacity did not exhibit interference, because our sensing mechanism is via a dynamic quenching not on the oxidizing capacity. The sensing of ClO− over other reactive oxygen species is quite worthy in medicine. For example, we can use B-CDs to detect the activity of myeloperoxidase (biomarkers for many diseases such as cardiovascular disease) [38–40], which can catalyze the reaction H2O2 + Cl− → ClO−+H2O, because the reactant H2O2 did not interfere with the detection. Thus far, Eliana et al proposed a new kind of CDs prepared with hydrothermal treatment of 3-aminophenylboronic acid and alizarin red S, which can detect ClO− with the presence of H2O2 [41]. But whether CrO7 2−and IO3 − can interfere with the detection remain unknown. Therefore, our assay has great potential for applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: . The validation of our assay in water sample detection
ce:section.ce:para: Disinfection of drinking water has always been a difficult problem [42]. At present, many countries utilized chlorination to eliminate contamination from bacteria and other microorganisms [43]. The free residual chlorine (referring to ClO−, HClO) in water is the effective ingredient, having fast sterilization efficiency and strong bactericidal capacity. The number of bacteria in tap water is inversely proportional to the content of free residual chlorine, so its content in tap water can measure the effects on water disinfection and can also be a sign of recontamination [44]. When free chlorine content in the tap water is too low, the tap water will probably be polluted again, unable to meet the water quality requirements; but when the content is too high, chlorine will be wasted, the smell of chlorine in the water will be unaccepted, and it will harm the human health. China sanitary standards for drinking water stipulate that the free residual chlorine content should not be lower than 0.3 mg/L for the water in centralized water supply factory, and the water at the end of the pipe network should not be lower than 0.05 mg/L(calculated as Cl2) [45]. We collected the tap water from a local drinking water factory and students' dormitory and pond water in our university. The detected results are shown in Table 2 . The contents in tap water from the factory and the student dormitory are 12.4 µM (0.44 mg/L calculated as Cl2) and 2.0 µM (0.071 mg/L calculated as Cl2), respectively. The values are comparable to the results from portable residual chlorine detector (0.48 mg/L and 0.82 mg/L for tap water in waterworks and pipe end). The recovery rate of hypochlorites in drinking water was 98.2%-104.3% with arelative standard deviation (RSD) of less than 5.7%. The results prove that the B-CDs we electrochemically prepared are effective as reagents for hypochlorite detection.

Via an electrochemical method, a new kind of blue-fluorescent B-CDs were prepared by using DP and EGCG as carbon sources with a molar ratio of 1:1 for hypochlorite sensing. It was proposed DP would react with EGCG at the anode first and then B-CDs were formed through polymerization, dehydration, and carbonization. The B-CDs have the maximum excitation and emission wavelength at 372 nm and 462 nm, with a diameter of 5.5 nm. The presence of ClO− would quench the fluorescence of B-CDs via a dynamic mechanism. Under optimum conditions, a linear range for ClO− detection was realized over the range of 0.5–50 μM, with ΔF/F0 = 0.0056 + 0.0194CClO −, R2 = 0.997, and a LOD of 0.23 µM. B-CDs have high selectivity toward ClO− over other oxidizing agents including CrO7 2−, IO3 −, and H2O2. The method has been successfully applied to the detection of ClO− in drinking water samples. The recovery rate is between 98.2% and 104.3% with a RSD of less than 5.7%. In the future, B-CDs have great potentials as a valuable probe for disease diagnostics through evaluation of myeloperoxidase activity, which catalyzes the reaction between Cl− and H2O2 to form ClO−.

Tiantian Long: Investigation, Validation, Writing – original draft. Zhongyang Hu: Investigation, Writing – original draft. Ziyun Gao: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Hongmei Luo: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Hongchen Li: Conceptualization, Formal analysis. Yi Chen: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Lei Liu: Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Validation. Dong Xu: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by funding from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21505162, 31571874), Hunan Provincial Key Laboratory of Food Safety Monitoring, Early Warning (2020KFJJ02), Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province, China (2020RC3042, 2022SK2102), the Grain-Oil Process and Quality Control 2011 Collaborative and Innovative Grant from Hunan province, and Training Program for Excellent Young Innovators of Changsha (kq2009060).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Comparison of UV, peracetic acid and sodium hypochlorite treatment in the disinfection of urban wastewater
Pathogens
